results.possibleIdentity: James Healy was an instrumentation engineer at Grumman Aircraft Engineering Corporation (later Northrop Grumman) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Based in North Babylon, New York, he was an active technical collaborator with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to facilitate partnerships between artists and engineers. Healy's expertise in instrumentation was utilized in the E.A.T. technical registry, where he served as a resource for artists seeking to integrate electronic and aerospace technologies into their creative works. His professional career at Grumman coincided with major aerospace programs, including the Apollo Lunar Module and Skylab missions; a 1974 NASA chronology for the Skylab program specifically identifies a James Healy from the Long Island area (Bayport) as a contributor to hardware data. Alumni records from St. Francis Prep (Class of 1954) identify a James Healy as a retired Northrop Grumman employee residing in the Babylon area, consistent with the geographic and professional details provided.
